mnemonics.title: PDA Components Song
mnemonics.content: Q(ueens) S(it) G(o) P(lease) F(rom) - Q=states, S=Σ input, G=Γ stack, P=push/pop, F=final states

mnemonics.title: Stack Operations
mnemonics.content: PUSH - Put on top, POP - Take from top, TOP - Peek without removing

mnemonics.title: Acceptance Methods
mnemonics.content: FINAL STATE: End in F, Stack doesn't matter | EMPTY STACK: Stack = ε, State doesn't matter

acronyms.usage: The computational model with stack memory recognizing CFLs
Stack principle - last element pushed is first to be popped
Language class recognized by PDA (includes all regular languages)
PDA allowing multiple transitions, can handle ambiguity
PDA with at most one transition per configuration, handles DCFLs
Current configuration (q, w, γ) of PDA during computation
Set of all states reachable via ε-transitions from given states

visualTips.visualization: Picture a cafeteria plate dispenser: plates pushed in stack, last plate pushed is first to be taken (LIFO). Each 'a' read pushes counter, each 'b' pops to match.
Imagine checking if a suitcase is empty after packing - if empty at end, ACCEPT. Stack symbol Z₀ marks 'empty'.
Think of PDA as a ticking clock: state = minute hand position, input = countdown timer, stack = stack of papers to process.
Spring-loaded plate stack - each push adds pressure, each pop releases. The height tracks count (like n in 0ⁿ1ⁿ).

analogies.analogy: Stack is like a stack of plates in a cafeteria - you can only add or remove from the top. You push new plates on top, pop them off when needed.
Matching parentheses like () is like folding and unfolding nested envelopes - push each '(' as you see it, pop and match when seeing ')'.
DPDA is like following a strict recipe with one instruction per step. NPDA is like having multiple recipes and trying all to see which works.
CFLs are like languages where any noun phrase can be substituted - like 'the book' can replace 'the tall book' anywhere. Regular languages are simpler patterns.
